claim.content: The Hartree–Fock (HF) single-particle band for a homogeneous electron gas is analytically pathological in the thermodynamic limit, exhibiting a logarithmic divergence at the Fermi wavevector k_F, and in finite periodic simulation cells the HF band displays strong finite-size oscillations at wavevectors near k_F; diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) calculations recover a large fraction of the correlation energy but inherit residual HF-like oscillatory features close to k_F of sufficient magnitude to contaminate naive finite-N estimates of the derivative (dE/dk)_{k_F}. Therefore, reliable estimates of (dE/dk)_{k_F} from correlated DMC total-energy differences require sampling quasiparticle excitations at momenta removed from the immediate vicinity of k_F and extracting gradients from smooth fits to data sampled across a range of k values.

conclusion.content: The Hartree–Fock (HF) single-particle band for a homogeneous electron gas is analytically pathological in the thermodynamic limit, exhibiting a logarithmic divergence at the Fermi wavevector k_F, and in finite periodic simulation cells the HF band displays strong finite-size oscillations at wavevectors near k_F; diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) calculations recover a large fraction of the correlation energy but inherit residual HF-like oscillatory features close to k_F of sufficient magnitude to contaminate naive finite-N estimates of the derivative (dE/dk)_{k_F}. Therefore, reliable estimates of (dE/dk)_{k_F} from correlated DMC total-energy differences require sampling quasiparticle excitations at momenta removed from the immediate vicinity of k_F and extracting gradients from smooth fits to data sampled across a range of k values.

premises.content: The Hartree–Fock (HF) single-particle band for a three-dimensional electron gas is known to exhibit a logarithmic divergence at the Fermi wavevector k_F in the thermodynamic limit, and in finite periodic simulation cells this HF physics produces strong oscillations of the HF band at wavevectors near k_F; diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) calculations of E(k) in finite cells retrieve a large fraction of the correlation energy but retain residual finite-N HF-like oscillatory features near k_F of sufficient magnitude to contaminate naive finite-N estimates of the derivative (dE/dk)_{k_F} unless the DMC band is sampled at momenta removed from the immediate vicinity of k_F and a smooth band is fitted across a broad range of k values.

steps.reasoning:
Define the Hartree-Fock (HF) single-particle band as the band of single-particle energies obtained in HF theory, define the Fermi wavevector $k_{\mathrm{F}}$ as the wavevector of the Fermi surface for the given electron density parameter $r_{\rm s}$, and define the DMC energy band $\mathcal{E}(k)$ as the difference of total DMC energies when an electron is added to or removed from a plane-wave orbital of momentum $\mathbf{k}$.
[7]
Fig.1

State the HF pathology: in the thermodynamic limit (infinite system size) the HF single-particle band has a logarithmic divergence at $k_{\mathrm{F}}$; in finite simulation cells the HF band exhibits strong, rapid oscillations in wavevectors near $k_{\mathrm{F}}$. This pathological behavior is driven by the long range of the HF exchange hole producing incomplete screening of the Coulomb interaction.
[7]

Note that DMC retrieves a large fraction of the correlation energy but does not completely eliminate the HF-induced pathological behavior near $k_{\mathrm{F}}$, so near-$k_{\mathrm{F}}$ DMC band values can inherit residual HF-like oscillations and divergences in finite systems; therefore naive finite-size estimates of the derivative $(d\mathcal{E}/dk)_{k_{\mathrm{F}}}$ can be contaminated by these artifacts.
[28]

Define the quasiparticle effective mass by $m^{*}=k_{\rm F}\big/(d\mathcal{E}/dk\big)_{k_{\mathrm{F}}}$ and infer that an accurate evaluation of $(d\mathcal{E}/dk)_{k_{\mathrm{F}}}$ therefore requires sampling the DMC energy band $\mathcal{E}(k)$ at momenta $k$ that are sufficiently removed from the immediate vicinity of $k_{\mathrm{F}}$ so that HF-induced finite-size oscillations do not dominate the fitted smooth band from which the gradient at $k_{\mathrm{F}}$ is obtained.

Conclude that it is necessary, when using correlated DMC total-energy differences to obtain reliable gradients $(d\mathcal{E}/dk)_{k_{\mathrm{F}}}$ and hence $m^{*}$, to evaluate excitations at momenta away from the immediate neighborhood of the Fermi surface and to extract gradients from smooth fits to DMC band data sampled across a range of $k$ values rather than relying on near-$k_{\mathrm{F}}$ data alone.
Fig.1

motivating_questions.content: The determination of the quasiparticle velocity (and hence $m^{*}$) requires an accurate derivative of the interacting single-particle band at the Fermi surface. HF theory's pathological behavior near $k_{\mathrm{F}}$ contaminates naive finite-size estimates of the derivative, and even correlated methods that improve total energies can retain residual artifacts in the near-$k_{\mathrm{F}}$ region. Without recognizing these pathologies and sampling the band away from the immediate neighborhood of $k_{\mathrm{F}}$, one risks biased estimates of $(d\mathcal{E}/dk)_{k_{\mathrm{F}}}$ and thus of $m^{*}$.

paper:867767318044737939.authors: Sam Azadi | N.D. Drummond | W.M.C. Foulkes
paper:867767318044737939.en_title: Quasiparticle Effective Mass of the Three-Dimensional Fermi Liquid by Quantum Monte Carlo
paper:867767318044737939.zh_title: 通过量子蒙特卡罗方法计算三维费米液体的准粒子有效质量
